refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) remains at or below the customer-defined E_target throughout the evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across the range of supported worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 is a high-level KPI requirement that must be decomposed into measurable, controllable functional requirements. The refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is a system-level property emerging from multiple subsystems (propulsion, hydraulics, teleoperation overhead, idle management, load optimization). The requirement explicitly bounds the evaluation context (nominal conditions, evaluation period, worksite scenarios) to enable testability and to distinguish energy efficiency from safety-critical functions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish a test protocol that measures total energy consumed (fuel, electrical) and total mass moved (payload + vehicle) over a defined evaluation period across representative worksite duty cycles. Compare measured energy_per_ton against E_target. Acceptance: measured ≤ E_target with statistical confidence (e.g., 95% CI).
sub_frs: FR-3.1: The propulsion subsystem shall minimize energy loss during power transmission and motion.
FR-3.2: The hydraulic subsystem shall minimize energy dissipation in fluid power generation, distribution, and actuation.
FR-3.3: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize energy overhead attributable to remote control, sensing, and communication.
FR-3.4: The vehicle control system shall optimize load distribution and motion planning to reduce unnecessary energy expenditure.
FR-3.5: The system shall monitor and report energy consumption metrics in real-time to enable operator awareness and decision-making.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a closed-loop energy management system that continuously monitors power draw across all subsystems, applies real-time optimization algorithms (load balancing, idle shutdown, efficient motion planning), and provides feedback to operators and remote supervisors to guide energy-conscious operation.

DP-B: Design the vehicle powertrain and hydraulic architecture with high-efficiency components (e.g., variable displacement pumps, regenerative circuits, efficient motors) and couple them with a supervisory control layer that enforces energy-optimal setpoints based on task requirements.

DP-C: Establish a multi-layer energy governance framework: (1) hardware selection for efficiency, (2) real-time monitoring and adaptive control, (3) operator training and incentive structures, (4) teleoperation protocol optimization to minimize communication overhead, and (5) post-mission analytics to identify efficiency gaps.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Establish a multi-layer energy governance framework: (1) hardware selection for efficiency, (2) real-time monitoring and adaptive control, (3) operator training and incentive structures, (4) teleoperation protocol optimization to minimize communication overhead, and (5) post-mission analytics to identify efficiency gaps.

potential_sources: Energy efficiency is coupled to safety (e.g., aggressive motion optimization may conflict with safe braking margins); mitigation: safety constraints are non-negotiable design boundaries.
Energy efficiency is coupled to operator workload (e.g., real-time feedback may increase cognitive load); mitigation: HMI design must balance information density with usability.

Energy efficiency is coupled to teleoperation latency and bandwidth (e.g., high-frequency control loops require low-latency links); mitigation: network architecture must support required control loop rates.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is a systems-level design pattern that integrates hardware, control, and human factors. It avoids over-specification of a single subsystem and instead establishes governance principles that can be realized across multiple implementation modules. This reduces coupling to specific component choices and increases adaptability.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Operators and remote supervisors will act on energy efficiency feedback and modify behavior accordingly. Risk: If incentive structures or training are inadequate, feedback alone may not drive efficiency gains. Mitigation: Establish clear KPI targets, training programs, and performance metrics.

Assumption: Real-time monitoring and adaptive control algorithms can be implemented with acceptable latency and computational overhead. Risk: If control loop latency exceeds safe limits or CPU overhead is excessive, system performance may degrade. Mitigation: Prototype and validate control algorithms early; establish latency budgets.

Assumption: Teleoperation protocol can be optimized without compromising safety or operator situational awareness. Risk: Aggressive bandwidth reduction may degrade video quality or sensor fidelity, increasing accident risk. Mitigation: Safety-critical telemetry (e.g., proximity sensors, emergency stop) must not be subject to optimization; only non-critical data streams are candidates for compression.

Assumption: Post-mission analytics will be actionable and will inform future design iterations. Risk: If data collection is incomplete or analytics are not integrated into design feedback loops, insights may not translate to improvements. Mitigation: Establish data governance and analytics infrastructure early; define feedback loops to design and operations teams.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-C is well-scoped for a system-level decomposition. Recommend proceeding to sub-level decomposition of each layer (hardware selection, real-time control, training, teleoperation optimization, analytics) to assign ownership and define interfaces. No revision needed at this level.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system boundary that integrates all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and orchestrates energy governance across them. It owns the energy management framework, real-time monitoring infrastructure, analytics pipeline, and operator feedback mechanisms. It does not own individual subsystem implementations but coordinates their energy-related behavior through defined interfaces.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Energy consumption metrics (power draw, energy_per_ton, efficiency KPI status) are streamed from NGHE Solution to external monitoring systems (Site Manager dashboards, analytics platforms). Frequency: real-time or near-real-time (e.g., 1 Hz).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Mission objectives and energy targets are provided to NGHE Solution by Site Manager or planning systems. NGHE Solution translates these into energy-constrained task plans and distributes them to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives safety alerts from Safety Services (e.g., proximity warnings, stability alerts) and must ensure that energy optimization does not compromise safety. NGHE Solution may also emit energy-related alerts (e.g., 'efficiency degradation detected') to Safety Services for situational awareness.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution coordinates with Teleoperation subsystem over Network Link to optimize communication overhead and ensure that teleoperation protocol efficiency is maintained. Bidirectional: NGHE Solution sends control directives and receives telemetry; Teleoperation sends operator commands and receives system state.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of the energy governance framework. It integrates monitoring, control, and feedback across all subsystems. Minimal diffusion: energy management logic is centralized here; subsystems implement energy-aware behaviors but do not own the overall governance policy.

Vehicle owns propulsion and hydraulic subsystems, which are primary energy consumers. However, Vehicle alone cannot achieve energy efficiency KPI without coordination from Teleoperation (communication overhead) and Safety Services (safety-constrained optimization). Energy governance logic should not be duplicated in Vehicle; instead, Vehicle should expose energy-related control parameters and telemetry to NGHE Solution.

Teleoperation is responsible for minimizing communication overhead and optimizing remote control protocols. However, teleoperation efficiency is only one component of overall energy efficiency. Teleoperation should not own the energy KPI; it should own protocol optimization and expose energy metrics (bandwidth, latency, power draw) to NGHE Solution.

Safety Services is not a primary owner of energy efficiency. However, Safety Services must be aware of energy optimization decisions to ensure they do not compromise safety. Safety Services should provide constraints (e.g., 'do not reduce braking margin below X') to NGHE Solution; NGHE Solution respects these constraints during optimization.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The energy governance framework (monitoring, real-time control, analytics, operator feedback) is best contained within NGHE Solution as a cross-cutting concern that coordinates behavior across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. This avoids duplication and ensures coherent energy policy. Sub-components (propulsion efficiency, hydraulic efficiency, teleoperation protocol optimization) are owned by their respective subsystems but are orchestrated by NGHE Solution.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution becomes a 'god module' that owns too much logic, it may become difficult to test, maintain, and evolve. Mitigation: Define clear interfaces between NGHE Solution and subsystems; use dependency injection and configuration to avoid tight coupling.

Risk: If energy monitoring and control logic is distributed across multiple modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), energy governance may become incoherent or contradictory. Mitigation: Establish NGHE Solution as the single source of truth for energy policy; subsystems implement energy-aware behaviors but do not make independent energy optimization decisions.

Risk: If real-time energy control loops have tight latency requirements, NGHE Solution may need to be co-located with Vehicle control systems, creating deployment constraints. Mitigation: Prototype and validate latency requirements early; consider edge computing or distributed control architectures if needed.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: NGHE Solution is the correct IM boundary for the energy governance framework. Recommend proceeding to sub-level decomposition to define the internal structure of NGHE Solution (e.g., Energy Monitor module, Adaptive Control module, Analytics module) and their interfaces to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. No revision needed at this level.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (multi-layer energy governance framework) directly addresses FR-NGHE-003 (energy efficiency KPI). The DP specifies a coherent approach that spans hardware selection, real-time control, human factors, teleoperation optimization, and analytics. The DP is neither over-specified (it does not mandate specific component choices) nor under-specified (it provides clear governance principles). The DP is testable: energy_per_ton can be measured and compared against E_target under the governance framework.

diffusion_risks: Risk: If energy monitoring and control logic is scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules, the governance framework becomes diffuse and difficult to maintain. Mitigation: NGHE Solution owns the framework; subsystems implement energy-aware behaviors but do not own governance logic.

Risk: If real-time control loops require tight coupling between NGHE Solution and Vehicle, deployment and testing complexity may increase. Mitigation: Define clear control interfaces (e.g., energy setpoints, efficiency constraints) to decouple NGHE Solution from Vehicle implementation details.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for DP-C. It integrates monitoring, control, and feedback across subsystems without duplicating logic. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) implement energy-aware behaviors but are orchestrated by NGHE Solution. This containment strategy minimizes diffusion and ensures coherent energy governance.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Energy efficiency KPI (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is measurable and verifiable through field testing over the agreed evaluation period. NGHE Solution will collect energy consumption and payload data, compute energy_per_ton, and compare against E_target. Verification is feasible if data collection infrastructure is in place and evaluation protocols are defined.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-003 is allocated to NGHE Solution, which owns the energy governance framework. Sub-FRs (propulsion efficiency, hydraulic efficiency, teleoperation optimization, load optimization, monitoring) are allocated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services respectively. Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Establish a detailed energy measurement and reporting protocol: define what energy sources are included (fuel, electrical, hydraulic), how energy is measured (sensors, calculations), and how energy_per_ton is computed. This protocol should be agreed upon with the customer and integrated into the evaluation plan.

Define energy targets and constraints for each subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) that, when met, ensure the overall energy_per_ton KPI is achieved. This requires system-level energy budgeting and trade-off analysis.
Establish a feedback loop from post-mission analytics to design and operations teams. Define how efficiency insights are captured, analyzed, and acted upon to drive continuous improvement.
Prototype and validate real-time energy control algorithms early to ensure latency and computational overhead are acceptable. Establish latency budgets and performance targets.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-003 is a high-level KPI requirement that has been successfully refined into a coherent DP (multi-layer energy governance framework) and assigned to NGHE Solution as the owning module. The FR-DP-IM chain is logically sound and passes all gate reviews. However, the requirement is not atomic: it depends on sub-FRs (propulsion efficiency, hydraulic efficiency, teleoperation optimization, load optimization, monitoring) that must be decomposed further to assign ownership, define interfaces, and establish verification criteria. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to make the design actionable and to allocate work to subsystem teams.
decision.justification_detail: The energy governance framework (DP-C) is a systems-level design pattern that coordinates behavior across multiple subsystems. To realize this pattern, each layer (hardware selection, real-time control, training, teleoperation optimization, analytics) must be decomposed into specific functional requirements and design parameters. For example, 'real-time control' must be further decomposed into 'adaptive load balancing', 'idle shutdown logic', 'motion planning optimization', etc. Each of these sub-requirements must be assigned to a specific module (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and verified through testing. Without this decomposition, the design remains abstract and difficult to implement.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-3.1: The propulsion subsystem shall minimize energy loss during power transmission and motion. [Assigned to Vehicle]
FR-3.2: The hydraulic subsystem shall minimize energy dissipation in fluid power generation, distribution, and actuation. [Assigned to Vehicle]
FR-3.3: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize energy overhead attributable to remote control, sensing, and communication. [Assigned to Teleoperation]
FR-3.4: The vehicle control system shall optimize load distribution and motion planning to reduce unnecessary energy expenditure. [Assigned to Vehicle]
FR-3.5: The system shall monitor and report energy consumption metrics in real-time to enable operator awareness and decision-making. [Assigned to NGHE Solution]

assumptions: Energy efficiency is measurable and verifiable through field testing over the agreed evaluation period.
Operators and remote supervisors will act on energy efficiency feedback and modify behavior accordingly.
Real-time monitoring and adaptive control algorithms can be implemented with acceptable latency and computational overhead.
Teleoperation protocol can be optimized without compromising safety or operator situational awareness.
Post-mission analytics will be actionable and will inform future design iterations.
Safety constraints are non-negotiable design boundaries and will not be compromised by energy optimization.
NGHE Solution can be implemented as a coherent module that coordinates energy governance across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services without becoming a 'god module'.
open_risks: Risk: If energy targets are not achievable with current technology or cost constraints, the KPI may not be met. Mitigation: Conduct early feasibility studies and trade-off analysis; establish realistic targets based on benchmarking and prototyping.

Risk: If real-time energy control loops have tight latency requirements, deployment and testing complexity may increase. Mitigation: Prototype and validate latency requirements early; establish latency budgets.

Risk: If operator training and incentive structures are inadequate, energy efficiency gains may not materialize. Mitigation: Establish clear KPI targets, training programs, and performance metrics; integrate training into the project plan.

Risk: If teleoperation protocol optimization reduces safety-critical telemetry fidelity, accident risk may increase. Mitigation: Safety-critical telemetry must not be subject to optimization; only non-critical data streams are candidates for compression.

Risk: If energy governance logic is distributed across multiple modules, coherence and maintainability may suffer. Mitigation: NGHE Solution owns the framework; subsystems implement energy-aware behaviors but do not own governance logic.
evidence_needed: Energy measurement protocol: Define what energy sources are included, how energy is measured, and how energy_per_ton is computed.
Energy targets and constraints for each subsystem: Define energy budgets that, when met, ensure the overall energy_per_ton KPI is achieved.
Feasibility study: Demonstrate that energy_per_ton ≤ E_target is achievable with current technology and cost constraints.
Prototype validation: Demonstrate that real-time energy control algorithms can be implemented with acceptable latency and computational overhead.
Operator training and incentive structure: Define how operators will be trained and incentivized to achieve energy efficiency targets.
Safety analysis: Demonstrate that energy optimization does not compromise safety; establish safety constraints that are non-negotiable.
Teleoperation protocol optimization: Demonstrate that teleoperation protocol can be optimized without compromising safety or operator situational awareness.
Post-mission analytics infrastructure: Establish data collection, storage, and analysis infrastructure to support continuous improvement.
